sentences.text: Are you bored, young lady?
You can take a nap if--
Whatever.
You ask me the questions.
And I'll try to cut-- to cut-- cut them short.
OK?
To cut my answers short.
Majdanek.
Majdanek, I was working with a kommando, what they called.
A work detail, a kommando, with a group of people, supervised by a German national who was a kapo.
His name was Kurt.
And he was one of the most decent men I ever met in any camp.
The most incredible.
This was my luck, too, really, to know him, to bump into this man.
He was a Berliner who was imprisoned for political activities, anti-Nazi.
And our job was to take apart optical instruments that were used in artillery, sights, things of that sort, from the First World War.
All this captured equipment from the Polish army.
To take all these instruments apart and sort them according to the metal.
Brass, copper, nickel, aluminum, et cetera, et cetera.
Screws separately.
And each element to be separated.
I, knowing German fluently-- write, read, speak, et cetera-- this kapo, Kurt, made me to be the secretary, the [GERMAN].
What was my job?
Of course, I had to work, too.
I was taking apart as-- I worked, as well as everybody else did, all day long.
Except at the end of the day, I had an additional function.
I had to weigh out each metal separately and submit a report to him, how much of each metal we realized that day.
You know.
And these were separate forms and all that.
And I had a couple other people helping me because there were some huge piles of metal had to be weighed out, had to be carried to a big scale.
And-- and carefully, you know, watched and all that.
This is-- this is what my job was.
At the same time, Kurt was the most generous individual you could have imagined.
Because the kapos always had the worst rep-- reputation in camps.
And there were some beasts among them, no question about it.
And how this man never got-- I mean, how this man could stay human as he was in camp is beyond me.
I could never stand.
He was just a-- [SIGHS] The biggest heart.
Just a-- an amazing individual.
He-- when he found out-- first-- first of all, he would bring food, extra food, whatever he could.
Because he realized that to exist on this food was impossible.
He would always throw in some bread, an apple, whatever he could.
One day he found out that I have a sister and two aunts working.
That is, in Majdanek.
He found out-- I know-- at that time, I already knew where they were.
They were working on the same field where I lived.
They lived on a different field.
But they worked on the same field where I lived, in a barrack, where they did weave artillery baskets.
They weaved the baskets for artillery shells, my sister and two aunts.
But we never had a chance to contact each other.
Because when they came-- came in, in the morning, I would be on my way out.
Therefore, we never saw each other.
Every once in a blue moon, she would wave from far away, while I was leaving, marching out to another field where we had our workplace, right?
And as soon as he found out that [?

Helena ?] is-- Hanka is working-- there were two aunts-- this man, twice a week, would smuggle in food to this place of work and hide it under the crate on which he was sitting, on which he sat while weaving those baskets.
The way he maneuvered this thing, it was absolutely in-- incredible.
And Hanka was getting the food.
She found that food.
Eventually, one day he succeeded in letting her know-- letting her know that he's my friend.
And that this food is coming from him.
And she should be looking under the crate every day.
Because he never knows when he'll be able to put something there.
I beg your pardon.

Well, anyway-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --this was-- there was this warehouse attached with all these International Red Cross food packages for Polish prisoners who were not alive any longer, or who were shipped to other camps.
But these packages were still coming in.
Like, once a month, maybe.
And they were accumulating in the warehouse.
And a lot of that food was being spoiled.
Of course, the SS guards would help that-- that-- themselves to the better things like sardines, maybe chocolates, sometimes.
So he had access to this.
And this is where he got some excellent items, out of these packages.
And this is what he would bring to Hanka.
And he put it under the crate.
And amazing is that the SS women never got wise to it.
And this went on for quite a few months.
Well, eventually at some point, it-- it stopped.
But in the meantime, Hanka told me that she was-- she said to me, if it weren't for this extra food, they wouldn't have made it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Amazing.
The same man, Kurt, the kapo, was killed in Mauthausen.
In the stone works, in the stone quarry.
OK.
This was April, '44.
The Russian front was getting too close.
And they decided to start evacuating Majdanek.
And I was on that transport to-- one morning they loaded us on trucks.
And they shipped us to Plaszow.
Yes.
Yeah.
Well, Hans Berger partly was a German national who was in the camp.
He was a-- he was working as a administrative clerk.
He was always well dressed and well taken care of.
And like the German prisoners, they were all privileged.
They had, you know, privileges.
But of course, I didn't know much about him.
Although I know that he always walked around like a Prussian officer.
He had this red neck and this healthy look about him.
The man could have been late 40s, maybe early 50s.
He was probably about my father's age, I would say.
But of course, I never knew anything about him of the fact that-- what I saw.
And sure enough, on this transport, there was this-- there was this scene where I got to know him.
There was a situation.
We were in this overcrowded car.
And at some point, he started to lecture on hygiene.
Everybody was absolutely starved and disgusted and depressed.
Because we didn't know where we were going, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said-- and he started to talk about hygiene.
And there were-- the-- the-- the conditions were just awful.
There was this pail standing in a corner of the car, or that car.
And every time, it would slob.
And every time the train would stop short, it was spilling.
Things would spill and people would get totally, I mean, degraded and-- and-- and disgusted about this whole thing.
And everybody was ready to give up, really.
And he kept preaching about how to keep healthy, how to keep clean, and all that.
And suddenly this man who was a Warsaw ghetto fighter, Schlomo, he probably overreacted a bit.
He came over to him and he gave him an argument.
And to make a long story short-- --this German fellow says to him, look, if you don't think I'm doing anything right, he said, you take over.
Now, let me see what you can do.
Well, they had this argument.
And eventually he-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The train stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think the train stopped because somebody escaped, I think it was the same train.
I'm not sure any longer, whether this was the transport or not.
I get so mixed up with these transports, from where to where.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Uh-- at some point, the train stopped and they pulled off-- They pulled them off.
We had to get-- we had to get out of the train.
Because they wanted to count us to see whether anybody escaped from our car.
And of course, nobody from our car did escape.
But there were other-- there were some other people who escaped from a different car.
They had partly lifted boards from the floor.
And they were able-- at the-- when the train was making a-- a turn, a curve, at the sharp curve, apparently some people were able to lower them-- themselves out of the car and escape.
And of course, they shot them.
Because the guards on the roof noticed.
Every-- every car had a guard sitting on the roof, or overlooking, so that they could see the ground around them.
In case of escapes, it was, simply, shoot them.
This is really what happened at that case-- at that time.
They stopped the train.
And they started pulling out people from every car and count them, to-- to see whether everybody is there, whether no more-- whether more people escaped.
And this is when he-- when he took a beating from the SS.
And when finally we got back into the car, he-- he was so dejected.
He sat, absolutely not the same man anymore.
They really beat him badly.
And Schlomo came over to him.
Oh, yeah.
So what actually he did, I remember.
He prided himself that he is a veteran of the First World War.
And he really knows-- he really knows how to behave and how to discipline yourself in order to be healthy and stay healthy and observe hygiene rules, et cetera.
And no one can really tell him that it isn't so, or whatever.
I mean, he really came out with it very strongly.
This is when this fellow, Schlomo, the ghetto fighter.
Well, of course.
Well, of course.
Absolutely.
And this is when Schlomo overreacted.
He came over to him and he gave him an argument.
He said, what-- what are you trying to tell me?
Blah, blah blah.
And he pulled out this little package in tissue paper.
And he started to unroll it.
And sure enough, came out a little black cross, a miniature.
He said, look.
This is the-- this is the cross with [? clusters, ?] relief of [? clusters, ?] which I got for my heroism.
For heroism, he said, the First World War.
I wasn't fooling you.
I-- he said it in Yiddish.
I'll quote him.
He says, smart German, [YIDDISH].
[LAUGHS]
Well, what does it mean precisely is that he-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] He has his bowel movement with crosses.
[LAUGHS] He-- he-- he lets crosses with his bowel movement.
Something of-- something of this sort.
Well, this is very insulting to him.
He just couldn't stand it.
And eventually, eventually, what happens later is that when he takes a beating and we got back into the car after the count, Schlomo comes over to him.
And he puts his hand around his shoulders.
And he said, look.
He said, I really apologize.
I wasn't-- I didn't mean all that.
And he says to him, you are-- he said, you deserve a medal, he says.
And I would like to give you another medal, you know, really, from all of us, he said.
You are a decent man.
You are a decent German.
Of all the Germans I have met, you are a decent German.
And this was a very interesting gesture.
Because suddenly everybody felt sorry for him.
And-- basically, he was a decent man.
And at some point, he told us a story that he-- that he was-- that he is married.
He's married to a Jewish woman.
And he's not Jewish.
Because we all thought he was Jewish.
But he was not really.
And he tells us this story.
He said, I am German.
I'm not Jewish, he said, except that my wife is Jewish.
And the two children are, he said.
But she is in Switzerland.
You know how I got here?
He says, you know how I got here?
Because, he says, I came to visit my sick mother in Germany.
I was already in Switzerland.
And he says, as soon as I came in, I could not go back.
And instead, he said, they imprisoned me.
Do you know why they imprisoned me?
Why I'm here?
Because I'm married to a Jewish woman.
And of course, we all felt absolutely disarmed after a confession like that, that he made.
How long-- yeah.
Well, these are sort of rare things, really.
Plaszow?
Plaszow is the camp that you saw in 'Schindler's List,' where Mr.
Goth takes pot shots at prisoners.
I was there for about a month, mainly in quarantine.
But we were employed doing all kinds of odd things, like digging holes and carrying things back and forth and all kinds of crazy things.
But I used to see, he'd come in at Goth, on this white horse every once in a while, riding through the camp.
Luckily, he didn't-- he couldn't ride a horse and take pot shots at the same time, apparently.
He couldn't aim very well.
But I remember him.
Next after Plaszow, we went to Wieliczka, which was not far from Plaszow, the oldest salt mines in Poland.
This was the summer of '44.
Probably June, '44.
End of May, early June, 1944.
We went to Wieliczka because at some level, they didn't dig any salt any longer.
And there were huge areas where Heinkel, a German air-- airplane manufacturer, was going to set up a plant to-- to produce airplane parts.
And we worked-- we were laying-- we were pouring the cement, to make floors, in order to be able to mount the machines.
And this is towards the end.
There were-- at some-- on some levels, they already brought in the machinery.
And sure enough, towards the end of-- towards, I would say, about-- shh, shh-- the beginning of August, the Russian front was getting too-- too near again.
And they decided to evacuate.
So at this point, I-- they just stopped the whole operation.
And they shipped us to Mauthausen.
This was August, '44.
In Mauthausen, we were in quarantine.
This was a stone quarry.
And I was there for three weeks.
And one day, while we were in quarantine, some Germans came in, SS came in.
And they wanted to find out whether any of us knows something about mechanics, lathe operators, or planer operators or any kind of a machine, drills, or whatever.
And of course, I-- I had this brilliant-- [LAUGHS] --brilliant idea.
And I told them, I'm a planer.
I used to operate a planer.
Which I really didn't.
But I watched someone else operate a planer.
And I thought it was so simple that I could probably do it too.
I remember when I was a kid on the way from school someplace there, there was a mechanical shop on-- in the neighborhood.
And I would sometimes, in the summertime, early summer, when the windows were open, I would stop there for a moment and look through and see what they're doing.
And there were lathes and planers and drills or whatnot.
And the planer was very close to the window, which I could observe with ease.
And I sort of was fascinated by the planer.
Because they were doing different shapes out of metal, you know?
And different parts to some machinery, apparently.
And I thought I could do it, too.
It's very easy.
And this is how I got out of Mauthausen.
And I got into Gusen Messerschmitt Airplane Plant.
This was August, '44.
And there were two Gusens.
First, they shipped me to Gusen One, for to be processed.
And then Gusen Two, which was the Messerschmitt plant, the airplane plant.
And this is-- was the last camp I was at.
Well, it was secret to a point.
It was kind of wild, really, what I did.
I don't know.
For some reason-- Well, first, I did operate a planer for a while.
Except that I was-- the planer was so bad.
This-- this planer had a fault.
For some reason, it wasn't leveled.
And I had a lot of problems.
They were breaking-- I was breaking a lot of knives.
The overseer, who was a civilian man, an Austrian man, a decent guy.
Apparently, he must have been decent, or else he wouldn't do all these things.
I had this luck with decent people, [LAUGHS] which is kind of rare.
He realized that I'm breaking too many knives.
And these knives were tipped with the hardest steel, vanadium, which they couldn't produce any longer, the Germans.
Because their plants got knocked out, bombed out.
They had to buy this.
As far as we knew, they had to purchase the steel from Sweden, which was very expensive.
Those knives were tipped with the vanadium.
Once you break a knife, you're out.
You know, you have to-- to-- to replace it.
And he was afraid that eventually, they'll shoot me for it.
So he one day-- this was late 1944, late fall of '44.
Messerschmitt realized that if he's not-- if they are not going to do something about food, some supplement, food rations, that at some point, nobody will be left to do the work.
People were dying like mad, left and right.
We were undernourished.
There was not enough food to survive and work that hard.
So they made this agreement with the SS administration, apparently, for them to supplement food.
And something like twice a week, they would bring in-- they would allow certain rations to all the special productive workers, that is.
Whatever, whoever they picked.
Every department would get so many.
They drew a list as to how many each department, like lathe operators, planer operators, sheet metal workers would get such and such.
And there was a variety.
Bread, the real bread that the Germans ate.
Can you imagine?
Not the camp bread.
You realize that the bread in camp had a lot of sawdust?
A lot, a lot of sawdust.
You could see it.
I mean, you took a piece of bread and you crumpled it in your hands and it almost as much sawdust as anything else had in it.
And-- so they had this agreement.
And there was like, soup, skim milk.
Skim milk for the people who were spraying the fuselages, you know?
I don't know why they were-- they worried about the lungs of the sprayers.
I mean, people were dying left and ride, being killed and shot, and died of all kinds of diseases.
And suddenly, these guys worry about-- about lungs of a man who sprays the fuselage.
It's just so stupid, I'll tell you.
And I was chosen, recommended by this overseer from the planers, to-- to take care of the food problem.
Because I-- that is to-- because this food would have to be brought in from the Messerschmidt warehouse, which was right outside, maybe half a mile away from the plant, Sankt Georgen, in Austria.
And somebody would have to go there and get all these rations and bring them back and to distribute them to-- to the departments.
And he thought I could do it.
My German is [GERMAN], he said.
And sure enough, he recommended me highly.
Because he wanted to get me away from those planers.
Or else I would've been a dead duck, if I stayed any longer.
And sure enough, it worked.
And this is how I got involved.
And they gave me a few-- they attached half a dozen ration prisoners of war with me, to help me.
I couldn't carry all this stuff alone.
We had a four-wheel wagon with crates of the bread.
And the tanks for soup, and a tank for milk.
And it was absolutely a-- a new life.
OK?
And of course, went into Sankt Georgen the first time to fetch the bread.
And there was something, I recall, 140 loaves of bread I-- we had to bring in that day.
And a-- and-- and a thermal tank of soup.
Anyway, and so there was this barrack, the warehouse, with the bread.
And it was all supervised by German air force personnel.
And I was heading the kommando.
Of course, I was the one to come in.
And there was this sergeant from the air force.
And I told him-- I gave my request.
I had a list.
I had a piece of paper for him to issue, you know, sign by the people at the other end.
And he says to me, well, how do you want to do it?
How do you want to carry the bread out of here?
And that barrack was high up on stilts.
And there was this area, empty area outside.
And I said-- and my people were there with the wagon.
And I said, why don't we open a window and I'll throw the bread out? you know?
I'll throw-- it will be easier than to come in and carry it up and down.
And he said, OK, you can do that.
So we opened the-- the-- the window.
And I would-- and he would stand by me.
And I would simply take the bread off the shelves, two loaves at a time.
And I would count.
One, two.
Each time, I threw out two breads.
And my people would catch them and put them in the crate.
That was a very simple matter.
And he liked the idea.
And he stood there.
And at some point, he got bored.
He would walk away to the other end of the barrack because some other people came in for something.
And I kept throwing bread without counting loud, you see?
So this is how we had six or eight loaves more than we should have.
And then I thought, God, what am I going to do with eight loaves of bread extra?
They'll-- they'll shoot us.
They'll kill us.
We cannot simply eat them.
We cannot take them with us to camp because it'll be visible.
You cannot take a loaf of bread and stuck-- and stick it in your pocket.
All-- so-- anyway, on the way back to the plant, I had this little area where the-- the bread had to be cut, distributed according to the list, to the department.
Let's say the sheet metal workers were getting 40 portions.
The other were getting 22 portions, et cetera, et cetera.
We have to cut them up and sort them into those cartons, boxes, and put in-- and mark the cartons as to where it goes.
Then of course, so-- and then I hid two loaves of bread.
And the rest of it, I cut up, and I distributed among these departments.
I added four portions here.
Bread had-- one loaf of bread had five portions.
It had to be cut into five portions, OK?
So that I added a few portions to each.
And this kept on going for the longest time.
And I thought, well, I could-- if they catch me, I made a mistake.
I got carried away.
Instead of 71, I counted 70 or 73.
I counted 72, or whatever.
I figure, well, in the meantime, we had enough for ourselves.
That is, the group of-- our group, who carried that stuff, you know, who-- who handled the food.
And this went on for the longest time.
Until one day, I get accosted by this Russian prisoner, who stayed in the same barrack I did.
I already knew him.
Yes.
I already knew him.
I already knew Andre.
Because when I-- when I sprained my ankle on the way to the train one morning, I stepped in some-- I put my foot in some rut and I twisted my ankle.
And it was painful.
And he was the man behind me with his friends.
We were walking, like, early morning to the train, to be-- we had to go by train to the Messerschmitt plant.
It was like a 50-minute ride from where we were in camp, in Gusen Two.
The plant was located in Sankt Georgen, a little town.
And the plant was dug in tunnels under a huge mountain of sand.
OK?
And so when he noticed that that I am limping, he went over to-- he came over to me.
And he asked me, what happened to your leg?
And I said, I don't know.
I just-- I think I sprained my ankle.
So he called over his friend.
And they picked me up and they carried me into the train by my elbows.
And this is how they carried me.
This is how we got acquainted.
And eventually he is-- months-- some months later, not very long later, I find out that-- that he is the man who discovered that there is extra bread in all these departments.
And he was wondering where it came from.
So he knew that I am the-- the man who handles the food from the Messerschmidt, the-- the supplement rations.
So he says to me, that famous encounter we had in camp one day, on a Sunday, when-- this was-- this was like, early '45 already.
He says to me, I know what you were doing.
I-- we have been watching you.
[LAUGHS]
Well, the conspiracy was such that he claimed that they-- it-- decided-- but-- that they had been that from the beginning, from the establishing, from the beginning.
Because he worked on the tunnel.
He was already-- He was already engaged-- they were building that plant, the Messerschmitt plant, under the tunnel.
And he told me there were like 40,000 Russian prisoners died, working on that tunnel.
And-- and some other people, too, he said.
There were some other nationalities, but not as many.
They brought in Russian prisoners specifically to build the tunnel, the plant, the Messerschmitt plant.
And he told me that one thing he knows is that they have to survive.
And they also know that the Germans are not about to let them survive.
Because as soon as the Germans notice that they lose the war, that they are going to lose the war, he said, they're going to do away with us.
That's a fact.
That they've done it, he said, before.
And they'll do it again.
So that he said we decided that we are not-- we-- we are-- we are going to fight back.
We are not going to let them kill us.
And then he said to me-- and then he said to me, you are one of us.
So you can imagine how I felt.
[LAUGHS]
